text: Hi, I need a refill for metformin 500 milligrams twice daily and lisinopril 10 milligrams once daily. I only have five pills left, and my pharmacy said they still need approval from the office before they can fill it. I have been trying to stay on schedule because my blood sugars and blood pressure were finally improving this month.

After knee replacement last week, I was told ibuprofen 600 milligrams every 6 hours, but I might have taken it every 16 hours last night, and now the pain is worse. My knee feels stiff this morning, and I am not sure if I should correct the timing today or wait until the next scheduled dose. I also want to make sure I did not slow down my recovery by mixing up the instructions.

I started having new headaches and dizziness since increasing amlodipine to 10 milligrams in the morning, and I feel lightheaded when I stand up. The symptoms seem worse when I get out of bed or climb the stairs, and yesterday I had to sit down for a minute so I would not pass out. I want to know if this sounds like a side effect or if I should be seen sooner.

Before you prescribe cephalexin, please note I had lip swelling and hives about 30 minutes after my second dose last year. The urgent care doctor told me to stop it right away, and they said it might have been a true allergy rather than just an upset stomach. I have been nervous about antibiotics since then, so I wanted to mention it before anything new gets sent in.

My child took acetaminophen at 7 PM and then ibuprofen at 8 PM, and I want to make sure that spacing was actually safe. She still has a low fever, and I do not want to give the next dose too early if I wrote the schedule down wrong. I wrote the times on the box flap in a hurry, so I am worried I may have mixed up which medicine came first. If the schedule needs to be reset tonight, I would rather fix it now than keep guessing.

Quick med list: metformin 500 milligrams twice daily, lisinopril 10 daily, atorvastatin 20 nightly, levothyroxine 75 micrograms mornings, warfarin 4 nightly, and insulin glargine 18 units at bedtime. I am reading this off the discharge sheet while I am walking to my next patient, so tell me if you need me to repeat any of that more slowly. The only recent change is the insulin dose, which was increased after dinner sugars kept running high.
